children.value: By the CLT, this sum of independent random variables approaches Gaussian with:

children.html: μ=λ+λd\mu = \lambda + \lambda_dμ=λ+λd​

children.value: The full sensor simulation builds a pixel value from first principles:
— not every photon produces a signal
electrons — accumulate even with no light
— electronic noise from the readout circuit
: clip to [0, full_well_capacity] — the sensor cannot count more than its capacity

children.html: [0,2bit_depth−1][0, 2^{\text{bit\_depth}} - 1][0,2bit_depth−1]

children.value: This model is what NLM, BM3D, and Wiener filtering exploit — they are designed assuming exactly this noise structure.
Key observations from the signal chain:
Shot noise (Poisson) dominates at this photon count
QE reduces the signal but preserves the Poisson shape
Dark current adds a small amount of extra Poisson noise
Read noise barely changes the shape (small σ relative to shot noise)
The total is well-approximated by Gaussian (by the CLT)
ADC quantisation creates discrete steps but doesn’t change the envelope
The Three Regimes of Sensor Noise

children.label: The Three Regimes of Sensor Noise

Simulate an Image Capture: The Noise Model Behind CV Denoising Algorithms

children.label: Simulate an Image Capture: The Noise Model Behind CV Denoising Algorithms

children.value: Understanding this noise model is what makes algorithms like
work — they exploit the statistical structure of the noise: Poisson shot noise at low signal, transitioning to Gaussian at higher signal levels.
What to look for in the gradient simulation:
Left edge: grainy (read-noise limited, low SNR)
This is exactly the image a denoising algorithm like NLM or BM3D receives as input.
is a chain of increasing abstraction. Each distribution is a limiting case of the one before it, and each simplification makes the math easier while preserving the essential statistics.
Photon counting is Poisson — and so are defect counts, keypoint counts, and any rare-event counting in images.
The Poisson distribution’s key property — variance equals mean (

children.html: σ2=λ\sigma^2 = \lambdaσ2=λ

children.value: ) — means that noise is signal-dependent: bright pixels are cleaner (relatively) than dark pixels.
The CLT is the reason Gaussian assumptions work in CV.

SIFT descriptor components, HOG histograms, Gaussian blur outputs, background subtraction models, and denoising algorithms all rely on the fact that sums of many independent contributions converge to Normal.
Every CV algorithm that assumes Gaussian noise is implicitly relying on this entire chain.
, you are exploiting the Bernoulli → Binomial → Poisson → Normal chain.
(bright pixels): information is lost, SNR collapses
